speaker: interviewer
text: It says-- it says the-- April the 11th, 1996. The survivor is Paul Oppenheimer. I'm Susan Fransman. The interview is taking place in Solihull, S-O-L-I-H-U-L-L, which is in the West Midlands, England. And the language of the interview is English. I'm Susan Fransman. It's April the 11th, 1996. I'm interviewing Paul Oppenheimer. And the interview is taking place in Solihull, West Midlands, England. Would you tell me your name and spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Paul Oppenheimer, O double P E-N-H-E-I-M-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have any other names or nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: 20th of September, 1928.

speaker: interviewer
text: That makes you how old today?

speaker: interviewee
text: 67 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berlin, in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Paul, can we start with some of the earliest memories that you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born, actually, in a hospital in the Charlottenburg area of Berlin. And we lived in Neu Tempelhof, which is near the airport. Even in those days, there was an airport in Berlin, one of the earliest airports, I suppose. So I do remember the flying that took place just very near where we lived. We actually lived in a street called [GERMAN]-- number 3 [GERMAN]. It's interesting that the street doesn't exist anymore. Because we went back to Berlin two years ago. And we saw the maps of Berlin of those days when it was there. And after the war, war, now, it isn't there anymore, just due to the bombing. In, in those early days, we remember happy days with our parents. And perhaps I ought to say a few words about my parents, first of all. My father was called Johann Felix Robert Oppenheimer. He was born in 1902-- 1901, sorry-- in Furth near Nuremberg. My mother was called Friederike, or Rita for short-- Furst, originally. And she was born in 1902 in Heidelberg. My parents met at Heidelberg University, where they both obtained PhDs, doctors of philosophy, in philosophy. And they met there. And they got married in 1926. And then they moved to Berlin. In Berlin, my father worked for a bank called Mendelssohn Bank, a Jewish bank. And he was some sort of a commercial attache in the bank there. From those early days-- perhaps I should add that there was another younger brother, Rudi, sometimes referred to as Ruti. He was born three years after me on the 1st of October, 1931. And most of the experiences that went through during the war, we did together. And he, in fact, remembered far more about all the things than I did. And I've learned a lot from him, really, in reminding me of all the things that we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So going back to Berlin, then. We remember going, for example, to the zoo. We, we happen to have loads of photographs from those olden days in Berlin, which survived the war. Because during the war, we gave all our documentation to friends who kept them for us. And we got them back after the war. So we have loads of photographs from those early days in Berlin with Ruti and me in the zoo, and in the back garden, and entertaining various visitors who came to see us, especially uncle and aunt, brother and sister of my father-- Uncle Rudi, Aunt [? Liesl. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: And one of the things that my father liked in particular was he was an early trainspotter. And we used to go to one of the main stations in Berlin called the Anhalter Bahnhof. That was a special treat for us at the weekend. We used to walk there and see all the big trains that went all over Europe in those days. So, say, the zoo was another feature that we often went to.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the zoo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Feeding the animals, maybe, and then things like that. And again, it's, it's all those photographs that we have from those days really that remind us of that. And we went to many other parts of Berlin. There were-- there were lakes and all sorts of sites to see. We also remember that we had very nice holidays in those days. We went to, to holiday, for example, to Marienbad in Czechoslovakia. And we went to Switzerland-- Lake Lucerne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who went with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It, it was my parents, Ruti, and me, the four of us, really. And we went to the Black Forest and those kinds of places. And life was really very nice. And eventually, I also went to school in Berlin, to a normal state school, not very far from where we lived. And I remember those things because I have the actual reports, the school reports, from those days. So they help to remind me. And I actually have a photograph in the class that I attended in 1935 in the state school in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you actually remember about being at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very much, except that I did pretty well at school, from what I can see from the reports. But I don't remember, for example, having any other Jewish classmates. In fact, I don't remember anything about Judaism from those days at all, which is rather interesting, in that I have amongst all those documents a report from a Jewish community school that I attended presumably on Sundays, and where I did get a religious instruction. But I don't remember anything about that at all. But, obviously, it must have happened, because I've got the report. And then my name's is on it. And so I must have done it. But whether I've blocked out [LAUGHS] all my Jewish connections from those early days, I don't believe so. I just don't remember anything. I don't remember ever having gone to a synagogue in Berlin, or having Friday night services at home in Berlin, Although, we may have done it. I, I found out all sorts of things later on. But I just can't remember any of those sort of things from Berlin. What, what we do remember is, of course, that we had the rise of the Nazis. And the main thing that we do remember is the bands marching through the streets, the SS-- the SA, the brown shirts, stormtroopers, marching through the street with bands and music. And, in fact, my brother, Ruti, he used to tag on behind like all the other children with these stormtroopers until my mother told him that he shouldn't do that because those people didn't really like us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember him doing that yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, but he says so. And, and I believe him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what, what memories do you actually have of the, the stormtroopers?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can only remember them now based on what I see in films, really. I, I wouldn't say that I personally could remember actually seeing them, you see. And also we lived in this area, Neu Templehof, which was not a very Jewish area. I've now found out that most Jews lived in, for example, Charlottenburg. And in the middle of Berlin, there were various areas of Berlin where most of the Jews lived. The, the German Jews, lived in certain areas. And the Eastern Jews, of which there were also very many in Berlin, they lived in the eastern side of Berlin. So-- but then where we lived, there were very few Jews in, in that particular area. So this is another conclusion why I think we were not very religious in those days. And I now would imagine, I would think, that we were very assimilated Germans, really, rather than Jews, which many of the Jews were in those days. I mean, they started the liberal and progressive movements in Germany of Judaism. And, and a lot of them, obviously, went further, more or less the secular Judaism, and then didn't observe any of it. And I would've thought we were more towards that side of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you become aware of your Jewishness?

speaker: interviewee
text: Perhaps [LAUGHS] in a negative way, in the fact that we were picked on by Hitler and the Nazis, and that we became subject to various laws, although we didn't have too much of it. But we, we saw the stormtroopers, and the fact that they didn't like Jews. And obviously, my parents made us aware of the fact that Hitler, who came to power in 1933-- so after that time, it became fairly clear that the regime didn't like Jews. And we were Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did that affect your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to say that it didn't affect me at all. Because I carried on in school-- in, in this state school, where I was perhaps the only Jew in the class or maybe one of the few Jews in the whole school kind of thing. But I can't remember ever having been picked on or beaten or anything like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in that way, I don't think it affected me at all. But obviously, it affected my parents kind of thing, because they could understand what was happening-- the more and more things you were not allowed to do. And obviously, my father's job would be-- might be in jeopardy in the future. And more and more Jews were emigrating.

speaker: interviewee
text: For example, the uncle and aunt who visited us in Berlin, they both emigrated in 1933 kind of thing. My aunt went to Palestine, and the uncle went to London. So they, obviously, could see that life was not good in Berlin, and, and there was no future for Jews in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how your parents explained to you what the situation was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I, I, I just don't remember anything about those sort of things. I have learned recently now talking about it and all these sort of things, that we didn't talk much with our parents at all. I don't know whether this was us, or-- I tend to think that in those days you-- children just did as they were told. And they didn't have the free and open discussions that our children now have with us, where they are able to discuss everything. I'm trying to remember that we ever discussed anything with our parents, and certainly not important things like that, you know. They would discuss with us how we got on at school kind of thing, and what we were doing-- playing and, and that was it. I don't think they made us aware of all of the nasty things that were going on around us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember very much of the games that you played with Ruti and other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much in Berlin, but I do later on-- later in life. So I don't really know what exactly we played. We knew that there was a tram, the S-Baun that ran very close to our home. And we obviously traveled on that quite often. But otherwise, no, I mean I can't remember any games that I played at that time, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at home-- can you tell me something more about your, your life at home with your parents and your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little, I'm afraid, really. I just don't remember much about that at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about school? What memories do you have of school at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only that I liked school. I, I always enjoyed school. And I was always a very keen student. And I always did well at school in most subjects, except music. I was never any good at music and singing. But otherwise, in everything, I was keen and to the front and always liked to be top and all those sorts things, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In, in what sort of subjects were you top?

speaker: interviewee
text: Math and maybe even German language and many of the subjects, I think-- most of the things. I used to enjoy going to school and did well. And I think that's sort of been throughout the various schools that I attended over the next 10 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What school did you go to after that one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after that we move onto another place, really. But that was the only one in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Volksschule there, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you move on then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we're-- we then come to 1936. By that time, life was becoming more and more difficult for Jews. And, as I said, lots of them had emigrated from 1933 onward. So by 1936, it was getting later. And there were more and more restrictions about being able to take your money abroad, being able to take you furniture abroad, and all those kinds of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had to really get out quick before it got worse in Germany. But that was all really my parents. We, we, as children, Ruti and me, had no decision-- no influence on that at all. But were you aware of what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think by that time, we, we were aware, yes, that we were not popular.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you knew that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe the parents explained it to us. In some ways, maybe we heard it in the streets and then all those sort of things, yes. But there was no television. And, and I can't imagine that we ever listened to the radio. Maybe we listened to the radio occasionally to hear Hitler's broadcast or something like that--

speaker: interviewer
text: You said--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and hear that he didn't like Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: You, you said you were picked on in the streets. Can you tell me about an incident?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I-- I can't remember ever having been picked on on the streets, really. And yet I look fairly Jewish, I suppose, maybe not terribly typical. But I would have thought that would have picked me out. But, no, I think it just wasn't a very Jewish area. And there weren't many Jews living in Templehof. So we didn't have the difficult-- there weren't any-- there, there weren't many shops there in that particular area. And I should imagine there weren't very many Jewish shops in that area. It just wasn't a very Jewish area of Berlin there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after that? Where did you go next? You, you said that you changed--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --schools.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes, we moved on then. In, in the beginning of 1936, there had been a lot of correspondence between my father and his brother in England-- Uncle Rudi, not to be confused with my brother, Rudi-- I better call him 'Ruti.' So, they had correspondence. And they came to the conclusion that we ought to go to London, and we ought to leave Germany. And so in February 1936, my mother, Ruti, and me left Berlin. And we traveled by train to Ostend, and then by boat. And we arrived in London. And we stayed with this uncle and aunt, Rudi and Lotte in number 30, the avenue in northwest London in Willesden. They had a flat there. And we stayed with them. They didn't have any children at that time. They'd only recently gotten married. They had both been lawyers-- or they'd had degrees in law from Germany. But, of course, they were not allowed to practice law because the law was different. And anyway, it was a very restricted trade. So they were not allowed to practice. And my father-- my Uncle Rudi, he got a job in the clothing trade-- rag trade in England and made his way from there. The interesting thing is that my father did not come with us to England. And that, in retrospect, of course, was a major mistake. We have never been able to find out why he didn't come with us. It may be that he couldn't get a visa to come to England. It may be that he couldn't get a work permit to come to England. It may be that we had to pay a lot of money for him to come to England. There are all sorts of things that I've heard now, which were the rules, at that time, in Britain for-- asylum seekers is the word now and refugees. And it's very interesting to compare our situation in 1936 with what's going on today. But one way or another, my father did not come with us to London. And, and this is, again, one of these things that we never discussed with our parents-- why he didn't come. And we also didn't discuss them with our Uncle Rudi and Lotte, which, which was a great shame because they only died a couple of years ago. And, and we should have really asked them all these kind of questions. But I didn't think it was important. We knew that he hadn't come. And I didn't think it was important why he hadn't come. But it is now because reciting the story and writing my book about it all. I wished I did know, definitely, why he didn't come to London. But he didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were left with Uncle Rudi and Lotte in, in London. And I, actually-- and Rudi-- went to a school, the West Hampstead Day School, a Jewish school, in London. And we had to learn, of course, a new language because we couldn't speak any English. And we made our way there at school. And we played.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I particularly admired Uncle Rudi. Uncle Rudi, in many ways, was my role model. My father was an intellectual and an academic and not a practical man and not a sportsman. Not that there's anything wrong with being any of those kinds of things, but that's what I-- Uncle Rudi was exactly the opposite. He was a sportsman and a practical man. And he loved cars, for example. And then we-- I actually have the receipts here-- the number of cars that he bought for 5 pounds and 3 pounds and sold for 10 pounds kind of thing. In particular, I remember when we were there in 1936-- and we have a photograph here-- is of a Bullnose Morris Cowley that we had with a dickey seat at the back thing. And we toured around-- he, he, he drove us around London in that. And there were very few cars in those days, really. And, and it was good. And he showed as all his photographs of when he played football in goal for the Nuremberg University, where he'd studied. And he was a great swimmer. And he showed us photographs of him doing swallow dives from the highest Olympic boards. And all of those sorts of things he was really interested in. And England suited him to the grand because of the-- sports, you know, was always the overriding importance kind of thing. And the papers was always about sports, football and cricket and that. And he loved it until his dying days. He used to watch every weekend and Jimmy Hill. He used to ring up Jimmy Hill. And he used to talk about the match with him, you know. And he was the sort of person that I wanted to be. And, and maybe he had a big influence on me in those days in 1936. One of the particular things I remember in those days, in London, was the trolley buses. We'd never seen anything like a trolley bus, before. But they run very near to us in Willesden kind of thing. You'll remember those. And with Ruti, we used to play in the streets kind of thing, pretending to be trolley buses, with bits of cardboard in front of us and destinations-- [LAUGHS] boards on it, and those sort of things. And we had a good time in England. But, of course, we didn't have our father with us. We went to various friends and relatives of Uncle Rudi. And there, Ruti had the-- another hole in his head. I was always pushing him, and shoving him around. And he always had some bandages around his head kind of thing. The, the first time was in the back garden in Berlin, when I pushed him out of our sandpit. And he had a big hole in his head. And the second time was in Ostend when we're trying to catch the boat. And I pulled him along a bit too fast, I suppose. And he fell over. And he had another hole in his head. And then, in London, we were at some friends of Rudi and Lotte's who had a swimming pool in the background. And I pushed him into the swimming pool. And he had another hole in his head. And he was-- we always have pictures of him with a big bandage around his head. And I think now I was a bit hard on him, I suppose, in those days. But everything was all right there. The, the really major event that happened to us in London was that my sister, Eve, was born. On the 23rd of June, 1936, she was born. And she was christened-- or she was named Rachel Eve Dorothy Oppenheimer. And within the family, she has always and will always be called Eve. But, for some reason, in the Jewish survivor community in London, she's known as Rachel. I think she went to a place-- Lingfield House-- once, where there was another Eve. And in order to not mix up the two Eves, Eve one and Eve two, our Eve decided to be called Rachel. And she's now known by most people in the London survivor community as Rachel. So, excuse me if I carry on calling her Eve because it's very difficult for me to call her Rachel. I shall mix it all up kind of thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Eve was born. And having been born in London, she was British. Now, we always thought that she was British, just like that. But again, I've thought about that more recently. What we had, later on in the camps-- and if it wasn't for this business that Eve was born in London, I wouldn't be sitting here today.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she was a British citizen. We had her birth certificate. And I have a copy of it here amongst our documents. She never had a British passport. At the time, until quite recently, I hadn't realized that there was a difference between having a British passport and having a British birth certificate. But, at that time, we just assumed she was British. And, and that helped us enormously later on at the time. Now another question that we never asked our parents is whether this is why we went to London. Because, obviously, Eve was born in June. And we went to London in February. Obviously, my mother was pregnant by that time. And therefore, they knew that there was going to be a birth. Whether my parents decided to have the birth in England just because the conditions for a birth were better in England than in Germany, especially for Jews; whether there might have been some restrictions in hospitals in Berlin on Jews; or whether they really were-- had enough foresight to see that, later on in life, being born in Britain might make a difference, which it did-- whether they knew that, I don't know, another thing that we never asked. And, and we never asked our Uncle Rudi and Lotte either, whether they knew why we did that. But we did, fortunately.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, we stayed in London for about six months, really, with Uncle-- Lotte and Rudi. And then, my father had managed to get a job through his Mendelssohn Bank. They had a-- an offshoot of the bank, or an overseas branch, in Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father got a job at the Amsterdam branch of the Mendelssohn Bank. So, in September 1936, he asked us all to come and join him in Holland. And we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we'd only been in London, in England, for six months, really. I'd learned quite a lot of English and had been to school. And we said farewell to everybody-- to uncle and aunt, and all  of our friends, and to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we have letters from the headmaster, from the school, saying how sorry he is that we had to leave the school, and what good pupils we were. And we have our reports from the short time that we actually went to this West Hampstead Day School in London. And we left. And we went to Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you feel about leaving England to go back to-- or to go to Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm, I'm not great on feelings, as you will find out. But I would say that I was very disappointed that-- I liked it in England, really. And by that time, yes, they were playing sports, I suppose. And, generally, I liked Uncle Rudi and Lotte. Lotte, perhaps I should have said, was a-- was a great cook, especially in the cakes department, like the cake that we had at lunchtime today. That was probably from Lotte-- the recipe. And it was very nice. I, I enjoyed it. And I liked England. And, And I think I was disappointed. On the other hand, I suppose, we missed our father, although he was more of a disciplinarian and all that kind of stuff compared with Uncle Rudi. But, no, I think the family had to get together. And, obviously, my father, for some reason or another, couldn't come to England. I mean, he, he should have come and joined us, really, rather than us joining him in Holland. On the other hand, Holland was, in 1936, perhaps perceived to be reasonably OK because there was really no idea that Hitler and the Nazis would invade Holland, that in-- by 1936. In the first World War, Holland had remained neutral. They kept out of it-- out of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, even those people who thought that there might be another war with the Nazis and all that, nobody thought that Holland would get involved. So we thought we'd be fairly safe-- obviously, safer in England, but reasonably safe in Holland. So--
